data.title: Real estate stocks continue to post gains, outperform broader markets
data.description:
Real estate stocks continued their winning streak, outperforming broader markets to close the week on a positive note.

The S&P 500 Real Estate Index Sector (SP500-60) increased 1.31% to close at 287.01 points, while the accompanying State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) gained 1.48% to $45.36.

The Dow Jones REIT Indx Equity REIT Total Return Index (REIT:IND) rose 1.88%, while the FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs index advanced 1.74%.

Kimco Realty (KIM) led the largecap gainers, adding 6.93% W/W to $25.91.

The stock hit a 52-week high this week after pricing an upsized $525M 3.50% exchangeable senior notes offering. The notes saw strong investor demand, prompting Stifel Nicolaus to raise the price target to $28.

BXP (BXP) (+6.34% W/W to $66.28), Public Storage (PSA) (+5.25% W/W to $325.94), and KE Holdings (BEKE) (+5.04% W/W to $16.89) followed in the list.

Evercore ISI raised its price target on Public Storage to $317 from $306 to reflect recent sector data and valuation roll-forwards.

American Tower (AMT) (-3.58% W/W to $192.50) and CoStar Group (CSGP) (-3.10% W/W to $32.84) led the losers.

CSGP dipped as Google ((GOOG)) ((GOOGL)) said it is rolling out an enhanced version of Local Services Ads for Home Listings with real estate listings across all 50 states, linking homebuyers with realtors in one platform. The expansion of Google's move into the real estate market is weighing on shares of competitors.

Among midcap stocks, Fermi (FRMI) led the gainers, rising 23.53% week-over-week to $7.14. FRMI shares jumped after talk surfaced over OpenAI's AI infrastructure plans and drew traders to data-center builders with ready power.

Compass (COMP) (+11.41% W/W to $8.59) was the second biggest gainer.

CareTrust REIT (CTRE) was the top loser, retreating 2.95% week-over-week to $36.85.

Chatham Lodging Trust (CLDT) (+12.41% W/W to $12.86), Transcontinental Realty Investors (TCI) (+12.05% W/W to $41.47), Hudson Pacific Properties (HPP) (+11.28% W/W to $15.29), Hotel101 Global Holdings (HBNB) (-7.51% W/W to $5.24), and Apartment Investment and Management Company (AIV) (-4.95% W/W to $2.88) were the smallcap movers.

Here is a look at the subsector performances for the week:

data.title: Citi, American Express in gainers; Visa, Mastercard in losers: week's financials wrap
data.description:
Wall Street ended the week higher as stocks rebounded from an early-week selloff, while investors weighed the latest inflation data, ongoing developments in Middle East diplomacy, and the strong debut of SpaceX.

State Street Financial Sel Sec SPDR ETF (XLF) added ~2% to close the week at $53.34.

Among megacap stocks, Citigroup (C) saw the biggest jump in its stock price, advancing 5.56% week-over-week to close at $139.83.

Citi saw several positive developments during the course of the week, with a major boost coming from President Donald Trump congratulating the banking giant for ranking Number 1 in the M&A advisory market by value in Q1.

Furthermore, the company was said to be in discussions with some of the largest private companies for the rollout of tokenized shares. The banking giant was establishing a way for its wealthy and institutional clients to trade private company shares through a blockchain.

American Express (AXP) was the next on the list, adding 4.76% to $325.44. The stock rose after its CFO said he expects the card issuer's fee revenue to pick up in the rest of the year.

Visa (V) (-0.36% W/W to $322.39) and Mastercard (MA) (-0.22% W/W to $489.98) were the significant decliners in the category

Bill Gurley has issued a stark warning for the two stocks, arguing that stablecoins could pose a structural threat rather than just pressure their margins.

The week also saw a federal judge give a preliminary approval to the two companies' revised $38B settlement with merchants who had alleged that the card networks' swipe fees overcharged merchants to process payments on credit cards.

Galaxy Digital (GLXY) (+32.70% W/W to $33.36) led the largecap gainers. Crypto-linked equities pushed higher as a rebound in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) prices injected fresh bullish sentiment back into the digital asset sector.

TransUnion (TRU) (-6.41% W/W to $66.13) and Pershing Square (PS) (-6.15% W/W to $33.55) were major decliners in the category.

Maase (MAAS) (+39.40% W/W to $14.86) and Intercorp Financial Services (IFS) (+20.65% W/W to $56.85) led the midcap gainers.

data.title: China opposes Pentagon move against top firms including Alibaba, Baidu, Nio
data.description:
China on Saturday raised concerns over the Pentagon's recent decision to designate some of its leading tech firms, including Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU), as Chinese military firms, calling on the Trump administration to halt the move immediately.

Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU) traded lower on Monday after the U.S. Defense Department added the tech giants, along with several other top Chinese firms, including BYD (BYDDF) (BYDDY) and NIO (NIO), to a list of Chinese military companies operating in the U.S.

“China is strongly dissatisfied and firmly opposes this,” the Chinese commerce ministry said in a statement.

“China urges the U.S. to immediately stop its erroneous practices, immediately withdraw relevant measures, and return to the correct track of building a constructive strategic and stable China-U.S. relationship.” China will “inevitably retaliate resolutely and forcefully” if its firms are not treated fairly in the U.S., the statement read.

The Pentagon's move came less than a month after President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping agreed to boost bilateral economic ties during a much-anticipated summit in Shanghai.

Under U.S. law, the Pentagon will be barred from contracting with the companies named in the list starting later this month and from buying their products or services through third parties beginning in June 2027.

By adding the firms, “the U.S. side has ignored the consensus reached during the meeting between the heads of state of the two countries in Beijing,” a spokesperson for the commerce ministry said.

data.title: Insider trades: Microsoft, Tesla, Pfizer among notable names
data.description:
Insider trades were seen across major firms like Tesla (TSLA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Pfizer (PFE) this week. The following is a list of transactions that occurred between June 1 and June 5.

  • Tesla (TSLA) Chief Financial Officer Vaibhav Taneja sold 2,605.5 shares at $402.20 each for around $1.05M. The transaction represented about 1.92% of his holdings, leaving him with 133,039 shares.
  • Microsoft (MSFT) EVP and Chief Marketing Officer Takeshi Numoto disposed of 2,500 shares at $412.45 apiece for $1.03M. The sale accounted for roughly 4.59% of his holdings, reducing his stake to 51,968 shares.
  • Pfizer (PFE) SVP and Controller Jennifer Damico sold 2,000 shares at $25.70 each for $51,400. The transaction represented ~5.99% of her holdings, leaving her with 31,397 shares.
  • CrowdStrike (CRWD) director Gerhard Watzinger trimmed about 8.62% of his stake by selling 7,500 shares at $700 each, with the transaction valued at $5.25M. His remaining holdings stood at 79,475 shares.
  • Medtronic (MDT) EVP and President of Cardiovascular, Harry Kiil, sold 4,189 shares at $80.44 each for $336,965. The sale accounted for roughly 10.11% of his holdings, reducing his position to 37,227 shares.
  • Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) Chairman and CEO Che-Chia Wei purchased 150 shares at $76.01 each for $11,402, increasing his holdings to 8.45M shares.
  • Robinhood Markets (HOOD) director Malka Meyer acquired 250,000 shares at $80.74 apiece in a transaction valued at $20.18M. Following the purchase, her stake rose to 8M shares.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Pfizer, AMD and Intel among top picks
data.description:
Major market indexes on Wall Street ended higher on Friday as the highly anticipated debut of SpaceX grabbed investors' attention.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Pfizer upgraded by RBC on risk-reward setup

RBC upgraded Pfizer (PFE) to Sector Perform from Underperform, citing a balanced risk-reward setup for the stock following a selloff from its 2026 peak.

Analyst Trung Huynh noted that while their overall thesis on the pharma giant remains largely unchanged, its stock has lost 11% from its 2026 highs to trade in line with RBC's one-year forward PE valuation of 9x.

“We think the risk-reward is now more balanced going into two key catalysts,” Huynh wrote, referring to late-stage trial readouts expected for Pfizer’s (PFE) lung cancer therapy sigvotatug vedotin and prostate cancer therapy mevrometostat in mid-2026 and H2 2026, respectively.

Intel gets double upgrade from BofA

BofA double upgraded Intel (INTC) to Buy from Underperform and raised the price target on the stock to $135 from $96.

Analysts led by Vivek Arya said they have a higher confidence in Intel's opportunity to help address industry constraints in leading edge wafers/packaging, plus supply into a much larger agentic CPU Total Addressable Market, or TAM.

Nike downgraded on longer than expected turnaround

With a more conservative outlook on Nike’s (NKE) growth prospects, RBC downgraded the stock to Sector Perform from Outperform and lowered target price by 29% to $50.

Investors will need to be patient, said RBC, as Nike’s turnaround under CEO Elliott Hill is making progress but is slower and narrower than anticipated.

“One of the main frustrations with the Nike equity story is a perceived slowness in execution for us and some investors, which perhaps reflects the size of the Nike business (supertanker vs. dinghy), which simply takes longer to maneuver,” the analyst team said, noting that the product lead time (sketch to store) in the sportswear industry is 12-18 months.

AMD gets Citi upgrade on GPU potentialCiti upgraded AMD (AMD) to Buy from Neutral and upped price target to $575 from $460, citing its GPU potential.

“While we have been constructive on the inflection in agentic AI led CPU demand and AMD to be the major beneficiary of it (see note), we now see AMD emerging as a legit second source in the GPU market with company poised to win lion’s share at Meta,” analyst Atif Malik wrote. 

The brokerage expects AMD to be the “key beneficiary of the CPU renaissance due to its performance leadership, greater core counts, X86 ISA, broader SKU offerings, and support for both multithreading and single threading.”

J.P. Morgan upgraded Illumina (ILMN) to Overweight from Neutral and raised its December 2026 price target to $185 from $125, citing a favorable outlook for the gene sequencing company following a recent customer survey.

KLA Corp. (KLAC), Lam Research (LRCX), Applied Materials (AMAT), and ASML (ASML) were in focus as Cantor Fitzgerald upped its price targets on the chip equipment makers, citing continued strong spending. The brokerage raised PT on KLA to $2,500 from $2,000, while Lam, Applied Materials and ASML were raised to $425, $650, and €2,000 from $320, $575, and €1,600, respectively.

Similarly, Mizuho raised the price targets on Sandisk (SNDK), Seagate Technology (STX), and Western Digital (WDC) as it continues to see AI driving a supply-demand imbalance in the memory market. The firm increased Sandisk's price target to $2,200 from $1,825, Seagate's price target to $1,090 from $875, and Western Digital to $685 from $550. Mizuho maintained its Outperform rating on all three stocks.

Despite a historic initial public offering, which has already seen some Wall Street firms praise the Elon Musk-led company, SpaceX (SPCX) received its first Sell rating on Friday, courtesy of investment firm CFRA. “We have a negative outlook given dependence on unproven outcomes including Starship commercialization, orbital AI compute, and xAI monetization,” CFRA analyst Keith Snyder wrote.

data.title: Sanofi type 1 diabetes therapy Tzield approved in U.S. for stage 3 of the disease
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has greenlighted Sanofi’s (SNY) type 1 diabetes therapy Tzield for expanded use in stage 3 of the disease under its accelerated approval pathway.

The CD3-targeting antibody was previously available in the U.S. for those aged 12 months and older with stage 2 T1D to delay the onset of stage 3 T1D, the phase at which the disease is typically diagnosed.

The latest approval expands its use to delay the decline in insulin production in children aged eight to 17 years who have recently been diagnosed with stage 3 T1D.

The decision was supported by data from the company’s PROTECT Phase 3 trial in which patients on Tzield could, on average, significantly slow the decline in C-peptide, a compound linked to endogenous insulin formation.

The French drugmaker is currently enrolling patients in its confirmatory BETA-PRESERVE Phase 3 study in a bid to convert the accelerated approval for this indication into a standard approval.

data.title: OpenAI under investigation by a group of state AGs: WSJ
data.description:
A group of state attorneys general has initiated an investigation into OpenAI (OPENAI), The Wall Street Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

In a subpoena from the New York attorney general, the company has been required to submit documents related to multiple business operations, including advertising, consumer retention, and handling of user data, some people said.

The subpoena served on Friday also sought information regarding OpenAI’s (OPENAI) activities related to minors and seniors as well as its deep learning models, model sycophancy, and company policies.

The Sam Altman-led company told WSJ it plans to work with AGs and considers their concerns seriously. “AI is a new and powerful technology, and we work every day to safely bring its benefits to people in a responsible way,” the company added.

“We take the concerns raised by state attorneys general seriously and intend to engage constructively with their offices.”

The investigation comes at a time when public scrutiny of artificial intelligence is rising. Earlier this month, Florida sued OpenAI and CEO Altman, accusing the company of releasing an unsafe product despite warnings about its adverse impact on users. 

OpenAI (OPENAI) didn’t immediately respond to a request for comments.

data.title: Notable tech headlines for the week: Apple, Oracle, Intel in focus
data.description:
The tech sector remained in the spotlight this week as investors focused on the blockbuster IPO from SpaceX, more earnings headlines as well as a revamped AI strategy from Apple.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closed 0.3% higher on Friday.

For the week, the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) fell 0.5% during the week. The top S&P 500 technology gainers and losers for the last week are as follows:

Top Gainers:

KLA Corp (KLAC) +31.94%

Sandisk (SNDK) +26.92%

Intel (INTC) +25.58%

Applied Materials (AMAT) +25.18%

Lam Research (LRCX) +20.95%

Top Losers:

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) -26.85%

Adobe (ADBE) -18.86%

PTC (PTC) -17.02%

Oracle (ORCL) -13.83%

Autodesk (ADSK) -13.71%

Here are some of the important technology stories from this week:

Apple launched long-awaited refresh to Siri 

Apple (AAPL) announced at its annual Worldwide Developers Conference that it is bringing Siri to the artificial intelligence chatbot age.

Mike Rockwell, Apple's Siri engineering vice president, said it is more capable and more conversational and has a “brand new design that reflects the power of Apple Intelligence.” The tech giant has already begun the process of monetizing it.

Wedbush believes the AI monetization and services will ultimately add $75 to $100 to Apple's stock and that is not being factored into the current multiple.

"WWDC 2026 illustrated clear progress on Apple's AI roadmap and suggested earlier monetization oppt'y than we expected," said Morgan Stanley analyst Erik Woodring.

Intel gets double upgrade from BofA

Intel (INTC) grabbed limelight this week after BofA double upgraded the chipmaker's stock to Buy from Underperform and raised PT to $135 from $96.

The brokerage said it has a higher confidence in Intel's opportunity to help address industry constraints in leading edge wafers/packaging, plus supply into a much larger agentic CPU Total Addressable Market, or TAM.

On the other hand, research firm SemiAnalysis said the Lip-Bu Tan-led company should consider raising additional capital to help with its turnaround and expansion efforts.

According to a report from The Information, Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Nvidia (NVDA) are considering Intel as a backup chip manufacturer. Alphabet's unit Google has reportedly ordered more than 3M Tensor Processing Units, or TPUs, from Intel. Nvidia has not yet placed an order with Intel. The company is testing if Intel technology can be used to make a forthcoming processor, the report added.

Oracle, Adobe in focus on earnings news

Oracle (ORCL) closed over 8% lower on Thursday after Q4 results, as analysts highlighted investor concerns over gross margin pressure and capital raise plans.

Oracle forecast higher FY27capex than analysts' estimates and said it expects to raise about $40B in debt and equity in fiscal 2027 to support its capital investment program.

Similarly, Adobe's (ADBE) second quarter results once again topped consensus estimates, but its CFO transition at the top combined with its continued freemium push on AI-enabled products create some uncertainties, prompting multiple downgrades from Wall Street analysts.

Other important news that was in focus included U.S. Department of Defense including tech giants Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU) within a list of Chinese military companies operating in the U.S.

Supermicro (SMCI) revealed a series of equity and equity-linked financing transactions totaling $7B to help it fund the purchase of components necessary to complete recent orders for its advanced AI servers.

In its historic IPO, SpaceX (SPCX) officially opened on the Nasdaq at $150, and ended the session on Friday with a gain of nearly 20%. The offering made Elon Musk, who leads both SpaceX and Tesla (TSLA), the world's first trillionaire.

Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said the recent selloff in U.S. semiconductor stocks should be viewed in the context of a much larger trend, stating that "We are at the outset of the AI revolution."

"We're at the beginning of it, and whatever happened to the stock market, you should be very happy because now you can buy at a discount. Everybody should be very excited." Huang told reporters in Seoul.

data.title: J&J multiple myeloma drug Talvey cuts mortality risk by up to 53% in late-stage trial
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) announced on Saturday that its bispecific T-cell engager Talvey, when used with Halozyme (HALO)-partnered Darzalex Faspro, reduced the risk of death by up to 53% compared to standard treatment in a late-stage trial for multiple myeloma.

Talvey, a GPRC5D-targeting bispecific antibody therapy, is already approved in the U.S. for the treatment of those with the blood cancer who have undergone at least four prior lines of therapy. Darzalex Faspro is a front-line multiple myeloma therapy.

The MonumenTAL-3 trial is designed to evaluate Talvey plus Darzalex Faspro with or without FDA-approved MM therapy pomalidomide versus Darzalex Faspro plus pomalidomide and dexamethasone (DPd) in patients with RRMM who have received at least a single line of therapy.

Talvey plus Darzalex Faspro, compared to the standard-of-care regimen, cut the risk of cancer progression or death and overall risk of death by up to 72% and 53%, respectively, J&J (JNJ) said, citing a median follow-up of roughly two years.

Additionally, at 24 months, about 89% of patients who received Talvey plus Darzalex Faspro were alive (overall survival) compared to 79% of those on standard of care, while 81% indicated progression-free survival ((PFS)) compared to 51% on standard of care.

The safety profiles of investigational arms were consistent with those of individual drugs, the company said, adding that MonumenTAL-3 is the first late-stage trial to demonstrate PFS superiority for a GPRC5D bispecific antibody combo for MM in an earlier-line setting.

J&J (JNJ) has already sought label expansions for Talvey plus Darzalex Faspro in the U.S. and EU, targeting RRMM patients who have previously received at least one line of therapy.

data.title: Anthropic takes latest AI models offline after government directive
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) is disabling access to its artificial intelligence models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, for all customers after the Trump administration issued a directive to prevent foreign nationals from accessing the latest AI systems on national security grounds.

The export control directive received at 5:21 pm ET applied to any foreign national, including its employees with an overseas nationality, the Amazon (AMZN)-backed AI startup said, adding that access to its other AI models will not be affected.

“The US government, citing national security authorities, has issued an export control directive to suspend all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 by any foreign national, whether inside or outside the United States, including foreign national Anthropic employees,” the company said.

“The net effect of this order is that we must abruptly disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all our customers to ensure compliance.”
